summary: I was raised in Miyajima by the sea, and that upbringing helped me when I traveled to America because I never got seasick. I used to go fishing there with my grandfather, which was a fond memory of my childhood. Miyajima was my home before I left Japan at age 16 in 1919 to join my father in America.

America was the country my father came to first, leaving Japan when he was 23. I came to America alone in 1919 at age 16 to help my father and study English. Life here was hard, especially with exclusion and feeling inferior at school, but I worked hard and lived with white families to learn English. America was a place of opportunity but also challenges, especially during the Great Depression when jobs were scarce.

Japan was my birthplace and where my family lived before coming to America. My father left Japan alone to work in America, and my mother came later with some of my siblings. I left Japan from Kobe in 1919 aboard the Korea Maru. Even after moving to America, Japan remained important to me, especially when I returned in 1931 to marry my wife.

I lived in Berkeley from 1919 until the war started and never lived anywhere else in America. My father worked in a laundry there, and I attended school in Berkeley, though there were very few Japanese students. Berkeley was where I experienced loneliness at school but also kindness from some teachers and neighbors. It was also where I started working as a schoolboy in white families to learn English.

My father was a very religious man and a member of the Free Methodist Church by 1919. As soon as I arrived in America, he took me to the church, which became an important part of my life. The church community was a source of support during those early years in Berkeley.

Kobe was the port city where my mother took me to board the ship to America in March 1919. I remember my mother and her sister seeing me off, and how my mother disappeared right after the ship's departure because it was too painful to say goodbye. Kobe was the last place I saw my family before starting my journey to America.

I traveled to America aboard the Korea Maru, a ship of the Toyo Steamship Company, in March 1919. The voyage took about 18 days, going from Kobe to Yokohama and then directly to San Francisco. It was my first long voyage, and I enjoyed it despite limited facilities, meeting many young people and picture brides on board.

The Toyo Steamship Company operated the Korea Maru, the ship I took from Kobe to America in 1919. This company was responsible for transporting many Japanese immigrants like me across the Pacific. The journey was long but manageable, and the company’s ship was the vessel that brought me to a new life in America.

After leaving Kobe, the Korea Maru stopped at Yokohama before heading straight to San Francisco. Yokohama was a key port in Japan for overseas travel, and I remember it as a brief stop on my journey to America. It was part of the route that connected my old life in Japan to my new life abroad.

San Francisco was the American port where I arrived after my voyage from Japan in 1919. From there, I went to Angel Island for processing before finally meeting my father again after many years. San Francisco was the gateway to my new life in America, though I soon moved to Berkeley where I lived and went to school.

When I arrived in San Francisco, I was taken to Angel Island for immigration processing and stayed there overnight. The experience was not difficult, but it was a strange place to me. I met my father there after about 7 or 8 years apart, which was a bittersweet reunion.

The Great Depression hit just as I returned to America with my wife in 1931. Jobs were very hard to find, and the automobile company where I had worked discharged employees. I had to take various jobs, including truck driving for a Japanese cleaner and delivering flowers in San Francisco, before starting my own cleaning business.

I attended a technical college in Oakland for about half a year to study automotive engineering before working as a mechanic. Oakland was where I gained skills that helped me find work before the Depression hit. It was part of my early adult life in America before I married and started a family.

I attended Berkeley High School after finishing junior high, though there were only about 14 or 15 Japanese students there at the time. School was lonely because there were so few Japanese, and I often felt inferior. Despite that, some white students and teachers were kind, and I stayed until the second year before quitting to work.
